topic: Someone discovers their friend has been lying about their job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lukewarm coffee tasted like ash in my mouth. I stared at the printed email, the font mocking me with its professional veneer. Sarah had told me for months she was a senior analyst at a prestigious firm, a title that promised a certain level of stability. Now, this document, forwarded casually in a group chat, detailed a completely different, much less impressive role. A knot tightened in my chest, a cold, heavy thing that made my breath catch. I didn't say anything immediately. Instead, I just folded the paper slowly, deliberately, the sound of the crisp edges rustling loud in the quiet apartment. When she finally walked in, whistling some upbeat tune, I kept my eyes fixed on the mug, my jaw tight. "So, what's the real story?" I asked, my voice flat, devoid of any warmth.

[story 2]

I watched her recount the supposed triumphs of her career, her voice too bright, too rehearsed. Every anecdote felt like a deliberate misdirection. My hands, resting on the counter, clenched into fists so tight my knuckles turned white. I let the silence stretch, letting the weight of the deception settle between us like thick, suffocating air. When she finished, a small, almost imperceptible tremor ran through my hand as I reached for the sugar, grinding the granules with unnecessary force. "That sounds... exhausting," I managed, the words clipped and sharp. I didn't look at her face; I focused instead on a smudge on the countertop, tracing its outline with my gaze, a silent judgment radiating from me.

[story 3]

The realization hit me not with a crash, but with a slow, sickening drop. It unfolded in the sterile light of the library, the details of her fabricated life laid bare in a spreadsheet. A slow, deep ache started in my core, a dull, pervasive soreness that made moving feel like an immense effort. I picked up my pen, but the plastic felt alien in my grip. When I finally looked up, my eyes met hers, and there was nothing there—no apology, no defense, just a vacant, unsettling calm. "I thought we were on the same page," I stated, the inflection in my voice dangerously level. I didn't raise my voice; it was the sheer stillness of my demeanor that seemed to amplify the accusation hanging in the air.
